overview: Lantern Control is a highly technical, slow-paced Modern strategy that focuses on milling the opponent's deck one card at a time to strictly control their draws. By leveraging low-cost artifacts and information-gathering tools, the pilot aims to win the first game by the clock, forcing the opponent into a disadvantageous tournament position. Despite its historical success and unique tactical depth, the deck fell out of favor due to powerful artifact hate and mana restrictions.
win_conditions: Milling the opponent's deck one card at a time to control their draws
Winning the first game by the clock/time limit
Sequencing incremental damage with Pyrite Spellbomb
Generating small portions of damage with Ghirapur Aether Grid

keep_criteria: Hands with 2-3 lands and at least one low-cost artifact or removal spell
Hands containing Lantern of Insight or multiple milling artifacts
Hands with early interaction like Thoughtseize or Abrupt Decay
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/shocklands if available, otherwise basics), plus at least one of Ensnaring Bridge, Thoughtseize, or Whir of Invention.
Keep hands with discard spells if paired with a land or a protection/removal spell.
mulligan_criteria: Hands with too many lands or zero artifacts
Hands lacking early game interaction or milling engines
Hands without a clear path to control the opponent's first few draws
Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple non-basic lands without fetch sources, or hands heavy on late-game threats with no early interaction or lock pieces.

examples.reason: Provides immediate artifact play, early discard to see opponent's hand, and a mana base to operate, aligning with the deck's slow, information-heavy game plan.

early_game: Play low-cost artifacts like Ghoulcaller’s Bell and Codex Shredder to begin milling
Use discard spells like Thoughtseize and Inquisition of Kozilek to see the opponent's hand and disrupt their game plan
Establish mana base and play Inventor’s Fair for consistency
Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize/Duress if mana allows, or play a basic land.
If opening with Bridge, play land then Bridge, then discard.
Turn 2: Play second land, cast Whir of Invention if Lantern is in play, or play another discard/bridge.
Sequence lands to ensure you can hit your 2-drop and 3-drop turns consistently.
Prioritize getting Bridge online by turn 2, as it immediately pressures and enables Whir recursion.
mid_game: Use Lantern of Insight to view the top card and control the opponent's draws one by one
Deploy Ensnaring Bridge to hold off attacks and buy time
Sequence milled cards to deny key combo pieces or lands based on the matchup
Hoard mana for interaction and Whir of Invention triggers.
Lantern of Insight is your primary card advantage engine
protect it and use it to dig through your deck.
Graveyard hate (Rest in Peace/Surgical) is crucial against combo decks
Avoid overcommitting with small creatures if the Bridge is still vulnerable or if you need mana for recursion.
late_game: Utilize Academy Ruins to loop graveyard artifacts and generate value
Sequence damage with Pyrite Spellbomb or Ghirapur Aether Grid to close out the game
Aim to win by the clock, leaving the opponent at a tournament disadvantage
Ensnaring Bridge lock combined with small creatures (Knight of the Reliquary, Thalia's Lieutenant, or discard-induced card advantage).
Whir of Invention to recur Lantern for infinite card draw if you have enough mana/lands.
Attrition through discard and graveyard hate until the opponent runs out of resources.
Go all-in when the Bridge is established, you have removal for their threats, and you can pressure with creatures or discard to empty their hand.
Also commit when you can chain Whir of Invention to generate lethal or win the game via card advantage.
Push for the win when the opponent has no sweepers or artifact hate in hand.

key_cards.name: Lantern of Insight
key_cards.notes: Grants the ability to see the opponent's top card, enabling precise draw control.

key_cards.name: Ghoulcaller’s Bell
key_cards.notes: Low-cost artifact that mills one card at a time to manipulate the opponent's deck.

key_cards.name: Codex Shredder
key_cards.notes: Works alongside other artifacts to mill cards slowly and control draws.

key_cards.name: Pyxis of Pandemonium
key_cards.notes: Provides additional milling capability to gradually deplete the opponent's deck.

key_cards.name: Abrupt Decay
key_cards.notes: Traditional control spell to remove key pieces or disable opponent's game evolution.

key_cards.name: Thoughtseize
key_cards.notes: Forces opponent to discard a card and reveals their hand to inform milling decisions.

key_cards.name: Ensnaring Bridge
key_cards.notes: Holds off enemy attacks and protects life total while delaying the game.

key_cards.name: Academy Ruins
key_cards.notes: Creates loops with graveyard artifacts to generate infinite value or damage.

key_cards.name: Inventor’s Fair
key_cards.notes: Searches for needed pieces and replaces artifacts sacrificed for their abilities.

key_cards.name: Pyrite Spellbomb
key_cards.notes: Sequences damage throughout turns to slowly drain the opponent's life total.

sequencing_tips: Know the combos, needs, and priorities of every deck you face by heart
Control the opponent's draws one by one using Lantern of Insight
Give more lands to Burn players, fewer lands to Amulet Titan players, and always give the same copy of Urza's Land to Tron players
Use Inventor’s Fair to replace artifacts after sacrificing them for their effects
Sequence milled cards to deny key combo pieces or force unfavorable draws
Common mistake: Overcommitting to Whir of Invention without enough mana to protect it or answer sweepers.
Another: Forgetting to use Thoughtseize on key combo pieces or removal on critical threats.
Unique interaction: Whir of Invention can target Lantern of Insight in your graveyard to recur it, enabling infinite card draw if you have enough lands/mana.
Also, Ensnaring Bridge can be targeted by Whir of Invention to recur it if it gets destroyed, but be careful of sacrifice outlets.
Always track the opponent's graveyard and hand size to make informed discard and recursion decisions.

matchups.advice: Give them more lands to slow them down and use sideboard cards like Leyline of Sanctity, Sun Droplet, and Pyroclasm for protection and creature removal.
Mill fewer lands to disrupt their mana base and use early discard to remove key pieces before they establish their combo.
Always mill the same copy of Urza's Land to prevent them from completing their mana trio.
Sideboard in Grafdigger’s Cage to prevent graveyard interactions and use removal on their threats.
Use Grafdigger’s Cage to lock down their graveyard strategy and remove key pieces with Abrupt Decay.
Sideboard in Welding Jar for protection, Pithing Needle for targeted hate, and Surgical Extraction for graveyard hate. Move Ensnaring Bridge out if they lack creatures.

Use discard to remove counterspells from hand. Cast spells at unexpected times (end step, after they tap out). Whir of Invention can be activated in response to counterspells if you have enough mana. Use cheap spells and value engines that are hard to counter or that provide immediate board impact.

Thoughtseize/Duress to remove ramp spells. Rest in Peace to stop graveyard recursion. Ensnaring Bridge to neutralize their large creatures. Surgical Extraction to remove key cards from the graveyard. Force them to play around your lock by holding up interaction.

Early discard to remove threats. Ensnaring Bridge to lock them out. Go for the Throat/Rest in Peace to handle small creatures. Don't overcommit to card draw; stabilize first and use the Bridge to stall until you can close out the game.

Pressure with Bridge and small creatures. Use discard to remove key control spells. Whir of Invention to generate card advantage and out-grind them. Force them to use removal on your lock pieces, then recur with Whir. Play around sweepers by holding up interaction or using discard to remove them from hand.

Win the discard war. Prioritize keeping Thoughtseize/Duress. Use Whir of Invention to recur Lantern for card advantage. Ensnaring Bridge is key; try to deploy it first. Trade removal efficiently and avoid overcommitting to creatures. Control the board state by forcing them to answer your lock pieces while you dig for more value.

common_threats: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), artifact destruction (Abrade, Shatter), and graveyard recursion (Reanimate, Flashback).
Note when opponents tap out for big spells
this is your window to deploy Bridge or Whir.
Track their hand size after Thoughtseize to know what they're holding and predict their next play.

overview_evidence.evidence_span: Lantern Control: a unique strategy in Modern's past, which could be used to control your opponent very efficiently... based on manipulating your opponent's deck... Its aim was spending the best of three's full time to win just the first game

win_conditions_evidence.evidence_span: Milling the opponent's deck one card at a time
Its aim was spending the best of three's full time to win just the first game
Pyrite Spellbomb , which is capable of sequencing damage throughout turns and leading the opponent through a slow health point loss
Ghirapur Aether Grid... idea is to work as an extra wincon, helping Lantern Control deal small portions of damage

sequencing_tips_evidence.evidence_span: you had to know, by heart, in and out, all other decks you might face
it is a deck with which you'll control your opponent's draws, one by one
give more lands to a Burn player, fewer lands to the Amulet Titan player, and always give the same copy of Urza's Land to the Tron player
helped us replace a piece we have sacrificed to use one of its other effects
help you decide what to mill and what to place as their top card in the deck

common_threats_evidence.evidence_span: War of the Spark brought us Karn the Great Creator... locked down activated abilities of all artifacts in play
Collector Ouphe works just like Karn the Great Creator... locked down activated abilities of all artifacts in play
Force of Vigor... can destroy two at once, greatly disrupting this strategy
release of Boseiju, Who Endures , a card capable of easily breaking artifacts
Mox Opal was banned just a few months later, which really made it hard for this deck to produce colorful mana
